risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Randomization to the three treatment groups was stratified by participant’s baseline chronic pain grade and recruitment source ... with blocking ... Block sizes were equal to 3 or 6, and were chosen randomly with 2/3 and 1/3 probability, respectively. ... The randomization list was prepared using the “sample” function of the S-PLUS® statistical software ..." Comment: This describes a clearly random sequence generation process, including random block sizes and computer/software-generated allocation, which is sufficient for low risk of bias.

Quote: "Treatment assignments were recorded on cards numbered consecutively within each stratum, and a study assistant not involved in the screening and randomization put the randomization assignments in sealed envelopes sequentially numbered by stratum. Randomization assignments were concealed to all study personnel with study participant contact until envelopes were opened by research staff at the time of randomization." Comment: This explicitly describes a sequentially numbered, sealed envelope system and states that assignments were concealed from personnel with participant contact until the time of randomization, which indicates the recruiter could not foresee the next assignment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Summary: Participants were explicitly told they would be randomized to COCT or one of two self-management treatments, and the interventions were visibly different (oral contraceptive medication vs in-person/telephone self-management sessions). The paper also notes that participants could identify cycle-focused content only in one arm: "At no time before or during study participation were participants informed that one and only one version focused on menstrual cycle-related changes." Comment: This indicates participants and personnel were not fully blinded, and knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence subjective outcomes such as pain, activity interference, depression, and treatment helpfulness.

Quote: "Participants completed the study measures at the time of study enrollment (baseline, prior to randomization) and at 6 and 12 months after randomization." and "The Graded Chronic Pain Scale (GCPS) was used to assess pain intensity and interference with usual daily activities." and "The 21-item Beck Depression Inventory (BDI) was used to assess depressive symptom severity." and "At the 6-month assessment, study participants rated improvement in their facial pain... [and] overall satisfaction with the treatment." Comment: The key outcomes were assessed by the participants themselves (self-report), not by a blinded independent assessor. Because participants were aware of their allocated intervention (e.g., self-management vs oral contraceptive therapy), knowledge of allocation could influence reporting of subjective outcomes such as pain intensity, activity interference, depression, and treatment helpfulness. Under the worst-outcome rule, the presence of these unblinded patient-reported/subjective outcomes makes detection bias high risk overall.

Quote: "Among the 191 women who were randomized, 20 were withdrawn from the study (15 of whom were randomized to the COCT group but then withdrawn due to medical contra-indications). In each of the three groups, 86% of participants completed at least one follow-up assessment... In the analysis sample, the three treatment groups did not differ significantly in rates of completion of both follow-up assessments (92% SMT, 98% TSMT, 92% COCT; P = 0.47)... Among the 25 women who completed the COCT protocol, 23 completed the 6-month questionnaire assessment prior to stopping the medication... In sum, among 74 women randomized to the COCT arm of the study, only 25 (34%) completed the entire COC protocol." Comment: Attrition is substantial and arm-specific. Although completion of both follow-up assessments was high among the analysis sample, the trial reports that only 34% of COCT participants completed the full medication protocol, with many withdrawals after randomization due to medical contraindications and side effects, and the paper also states that 24 randomized participants had no follow-up data. This level of missingness/exclusion and protocol discontinuation in one arm crosses hard attrition thresholds and creates a clear risk of attrition bias despite ITT and multiple imputation sensitivity analyses.
